description: The Linux ELF format used for executables, shared libraries, and object files. A compiled object file (.o) must have these structures added by the linker (ld or gold/lld) to become a runnable binary.

variants.name: ELF64 64-bit Static Executable (ET_EXEC, no shared libraries)
variants.description: Smallest runnable Linux 64-bit binary. No shared libraries, no dynamic linker. The kernel loads the segments directly and jumps to _start. All code and data must be compiled into the binary itself.

toolchain.assemble_and_link: nasm -f elf64 program.asm -o program.o && ld program.o -o program

required_components.name: ELF Header
required_components.description: Mandatory 64-byte header at the very start of the file. Identifies the file as ELF, specifies the target architecture, and points to the Program Header Table and entry point.

fields.name: e_phoff
fields.note: Offset of Program Header Table (immediately after ELF header)

fields.name: e_shoff
fields.note: Offset of Section Header Table; 0 if stripped

fields.name: e_phentsize
fields.note: Size of one Program Header entry = 56

fields.name: e_phnum
fields.note: Number of Program Header entries (at least 1 PT_LOAD)

fields.name: e_shentsize
fields.note: Size of one Section Header entry = 64

fields.name: e_shnum
fields.note: Number of Section Header entries; 0 if none

fields.name: e_shstrndx
fields.note: Index of .shstrtab section; SHN_UNDEF(0) if no section headers

code_example.source:
; === ELF64 Header (64 bytes at file offset 0x0000) ===
elf_header:
    db 0x7F, 'E', 'L', 'F'     ; e_ident magic
    db 2                        ; EI_CLASS  = ELFCLASS64
    db 1                        ; EI_DATA   = little-endian
    db 1                        ; EI_VERSION
    db 0                        ; EI_OSABI  = System V
    times 8 db 0                ; EI_ABIVERSION + padding
    dw 2                        ; e_type    = ET_EXEC
    dw 62                       ; e_machine = EM_X86_64
    dd 1                        ; e_version
    dq _start                   ; e_entry
    dq phdr_table               ; e_phoff  (Program Header Table offset)
    dq shdr_table               ; e_shoff  (Section Header Table offset; 0 if stripped)
    dd 0                        ; e_flags
    dw 64                       ; e_ehsize
    dw 56                       ; e_phentsize
    dw PHDR_COUNT               ; e_phnum
    dw 64                       ; e_shentsize
    dw SHDR_COUNT               ; e_shnum
    dw SHSTRNDX                 ; e_shstrndx

required_components.name: PT_LOAD Segment (read + execute, covers .text and .rodata)
required_components.offset_in_file: Program Header Table (immediately after ELF header at 0x0040)
required_components.description: Loadable segment for the code and read-only data. The kernel maps these pages as readable and executable (rx). Must include the ELF header and Program Header Table itself.

fields.name: p_offset
fields.note: File offset of this segment (usually 0 to include ELF headers)

fields.name: p_vaddr
fields.note: Virtual load address (conventional for static x86-64 ET_EXEC)

fields.name: p_paddr
fields.note: Physical address; ignored by Linux; set equal to p_vaddr

fields.name: p_memsz
fields.note: For rx segment: equal to p_filesz (no zero-fill needed)

fields.name: p_align
fields.note: 2 MB huge-page alignment (typical ld default); 0x1000 also valid

code_example.source:
; === PT_LOAD (rx) Program Header Entry (56 bytes) ===
phdr_rx:
    dd 1                        ; p_type   = PT_LOAD
    dd 5                        ; p_flags  = PF_R | PF_X
    dq 0                        ; p_offset (start of file)
    dq 0x400000                 ; p_vaddr
    dq 0x400000                 ; p_paddr
    dq RX_FILE_SIZE             ; p_filesz
    dq RX_FILE_SIZE             ; p_memsz
    dq 0x200000                 ; p_align  (2 MB)

required_components.name: PT_LOAD Segment (read + write, covers .data and .bss)
required_components.description: Loadable segment for mutable data. p_memsz > p_filesz if .bss is present; the kernel zero-fills the extra bytes. Omit if the program has no global variables.

fields.name: p_filesz
fields.note: Bytes of initialized data in file (.data size)

fields.name: p_memsz
fields.note: p_filesz + .bss size; extra bytes zero-filled by kernel

code_example.source:
; === PT_LOAD (rw) Program Header Entry (56 bytes) ===
phdr_rw:
    dd 1                        ; p_type   = PT_LOAD
    dd 6                        ; p_flags  = PF_R | PF_W
    dq DATA_FILE_OFFSET         ; p_offset
    dq DATA_VADDR               ; p_vaddr
    dq DATA_VADDR               ; p_paddr
    dq DATA_FILE_SIZE           ; p_filesz (.data only)
    dq DATA_FILE_SIZE + BSS_SIZE; p_memsz  (.data + .bss)
    dq 0x200000                 ; p_align

required_components.name: PT_GNU_STACK Segment
required_components.description: Informs the kernel of required stack permissions. Setting p_flags to PF_R|PF_W (no PF_X) enables a non-executable (NX) stack. Strongly recommended for security.

code_example.source:
; === PT_GNU_STACK Program Header Entry ===
phdr_gnu_stack:
    dd 0x6474E551               ; p_type   = PT_GNU_STACK
    dd 6                        ; p_flags  = PF_R | PF_W (NX)
    dq 0                        ; p_offset
    dq 0                        ; p_vaddr
    dq 0                        ; p_paddr
    dq 0                        ; p_filesz
    dq 0                        ; p_memsz
    dq 0x10                     ; p_align

required_components.name: .text Section Data
required_components.description: The compiled machine code. Must contain the _start symbol at the address stored in e_entry.

code_example.source:
; === .text section ===
section .text
global _start

_start:
    ; program code goes here

required_components.name: Entry Point Code (_start)
required_components.description: First instruction executed after the kernel loads the binary. The kernel pushes argc, argv[], envp[], and the auxiliary vector on the stack. _start must NOT return (no return address is pushed); it must call exit_group or invoke SYS_exit_group directly.

code_example.source:
; === Minimal 64-bit Static Entry Point ===
; Assemble: nasm -f elf64 program.asm -o program.o
; Link:     ld program.o -o program
section .data
    msg     db 'Hello, World!', 10   ; newline
    msglen  equ $ - msg

section .text
global _start

_start:
    ; write(STDOUT_FILENO=1, msg, msglen)
    mov     rax, 1              ; SYS_write
    mov     rdi, 1              ; fd = stdout
    lea     rsi, [rel msg]      ; buf
    mov     rdx, msglen         ; count
    syscall

    ; exit_group(0)
    mov     rax, 231            ; SYS_exit_group
    xor     rdi, rdi            ; status = 0
    syscall

variants.name: ELF64 64-bit Dynamically Linked Executable (ET_EXEC, shared libraries)
variants.description: 64-bit Linux .exec that uses shared libraries (libc.so.6, etc.). Requires a PT_INTERP segment pointing to the dynamic linker (/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2), a .dynamic section, PLT/GOT stubs, and symbol tables.

required_components.name: ELF Header
required_components.description: Same as elf64_static. e_type = ET_EXEC (2). e_entry points to _start (or the glibc entry stub if using libc startup).

required_components.name: PT_INTERP Segment
required_components.offset_in_file: Program Header Table (first or second entry)
required_components.description: Points to the dynamic linker path. Must appear before PT_LOAD segments. The kernel reads this path and loads the dynamic linker, which then performs all further setup.

fields.name: p_offset
fields.note: File offset of the interpreter path string

fields.name: p_vaddr
fields.note: Virtual address of the interpreter path string

fields.name: p_filesz
fields.note: Length of the path string including null terminator

code_example.source:
; === PT_INTERP Program Header Entry ===
phdr_interp:
    dd 3                        ; p_type   = PT_INTERP
    dd 4                        ; p_flags  = PF_R
    dq interp_str_offset        ; p_offset (file offset of interpreter path)
    dq interp_str_vaddr         ; p_vaddr
    dq interp_str_vaddr         ; p_paddr
    dq interp_str_size          ; p_filesz
    dq interp_str_size          ; p_memsz
    dq 1                        ; p_align

; .interp section data
interp_str:
    db '/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2', 0
interp_str_size equ $ - interp_str

required_components.name: PT_DYNAMIC Segment
required_components.description: Points to the .dynamic section, which lists all shared library dependencies and linking metadata.

fields.name: p_offset / p_vaddr
fields.note: File offset and VA of the .dynamic section

code_example.source:
; === PT_DYNAMIC Program Header Entry ===
phdr_dynamic:
    dd 2                        ; p_type   = PT_DYNAMIC
    dd 6                        ; p_flags  = PF_R | PF_W
    dq dynamic_offset           ; p_offset
    dq dynamic_vaddr            ; p_vaddr
    dq dynamic_vaddr            ; p_paddr
    dq dynamic_size             ; p_filesz
    dq dynamic_size             ; p_memsz
    dq 8                        ; p_align

required_components.name: .dynamic Section
required_components.size_bytes: variable (16 bytes per entry, terminated by DT_NULL)
required_components.description: Array of (tag, value) pairs describing all dynamic linking requirements. Must be terminated by a DT_NULL entry.

fields.name: DT_NEEDED
fields.note: String table offset of required library name (e.g. 'libc.so.6'); one entry per library

fields.name: DT_GNU_HASH
fields.note: VA of GNU hash table (faster lookup than DT_HASH)

fields.name: DT_PLTRELSZ
fields.note: Total size in bytes of PLT relocations

fields.name: DT_PLTREL
fields.note: Type of PLT relocation entries: 7 = DT_RELA

fields.name: DT_RELAENT
fields.note: Size of one Rela entry = 24 bytes

code_example.source:
; === .dynamic Section (array of Elf64_Dyn entries, 16 bytes each) ===
; Each entry: { Elf64_Sxword d_tag, Elf64_Xword d_val }
section .dynamic
dynamic:
    dq  1, dynstr_offset_libc   ; DT_NEEDED  'libc.so.6'
    dq  5, dynstr_vaddr         ; DT_STRTAB  -> .dynstr
    dq 10, dynstr_size          ; DT_STRSZ
    dq  6, dynsym_vaddr         ; DT_SYMTAB  -> .dynsym
    dq 11, 24                   ; DT_SYMENT  = 24
    dq  3, gotplt_vaddr         ; DT_PLTGOT  -> .got.plt
    dq  2, rela_plt_size        ; DT_PLTRELSZ
    dq 20, 7                    ; DT_PLTREL  = DT_RELA
    dq 23, rela_plt_vaddr       ; DT_JMPREL  -> .rela.plt
    dq  0, 0                    ; DT_NULL    (terminator)

required_components.name: .dynsym Section
required_components.description: Dynamic symbol table. One Elf64_Sym entry per imported (or exported) symbol. Imported symbols have st_shndx = SHN_UNDEF (0) and st_value = 0.

fields.name: st_value
fields.note: 0 for undefined symbols; filled by dynamic linker

code_example.source:
; === .dynsym Section (24 bytes per Elf64_Sym entry) ===
section .dynsym
; Entry 0 is always the null symbol
dynsym:
    dd 0, 0                     ; st_name, st_info|st_other|st_shndx
    dq 0, 0                     ; st_value, st_size

; Entry 1: import 'printf' from libc
    dd printf_name_offset       ; st_name  (offset in .dynstr)
    db 0x12                     ; st_info  = STB_GLOBAL | STT_FUNC
    db 0                        ; st_other = STV_DEFAULT
    dw 0                        ; st_shndx = SHN_UNDEF
    dq 0                        ; st_value = 0 (undefined)
    dq 0                        ; st_size  = 0

required_components.name: .dynstr Section
required_components.description: Null-terminated strings for all .dynsym symbol names and all DT_NEEDED library names. The first byte must be a null byte (empty string for the null symbol).

code_example.source:
; === .dynstr Section (null-terminated ASCII strings) ===
section .dynstr
dynstr:
    db 0                        ; offset 0: empty string (null symbol)
libc_name_offset equ $ - dynstr
    db 'libc.so.6', 0           ; DT_NEEDED library name
printf_name_offset equ $ - dynstr
    db 'printf', 0              ; symbol name for printf
puts_name_offset equ $ - dynstr
    db 'puts', 0                ; symbol name for puts
dynstr_end:

required_components.name: .got.plt Section
required_components.size_bytes: (3 + N) * 8 bytes (3 reserved entries + 1 per PLT function)
required_components.description: Global Offset Table for PLT lazy binding. First three entries are reserved: [0]=address of .dynamic, [1]=link_map pointer (filled by dynamic linker), [2]=_dl_runtime_resolve (filled by dynamic linker). Each subsequent entry initially holds the address of the corresponding PLT push instruction.

fields.name: GOT[0]
fields.note: Reserved; used by the dynamic linker

fields.name: GOT[1]
fields.note: Dynamic linker writes link_map pointer here

fields.name: GOT[3+]
fields.value: VA of PUSH in PLT stub
fields.note: Initially the next instruction in the PLT stub; replaced with real function VA on first call

code_example.source:
; === .got.plt Section ===
section .got.plt  write
got_plt:
    dq dynamic_vaddr            ; GOT[0] = &.dynamic
    dq 0                        ; GOT[1] = link_map  (filled by ld.so)
    dq 0                        ; GOT[2] = dl_resolve (filled by ld.so)
    ; Per-function slots (N entries):
printf_got:
    dq plt_printf_push          ; initially -> PUSH in PLT[printf]
puts_got:
    dq plt_puts_push            ; initially -> PUSH in PLT[puts]

required_components.name: .plt Section
required_components.size_bytes: (1 + N) * 16 bytes (resolver stub + one 16-byte stub per function)
required_components.description: Procedure Linkage Table. PLT[0] is the resolver stub that calls _dl_runtime_resolve. PLT[n] (n>=1) is the per-function lazy-binding stub.

code_example.source:
; === .plt Section ===
section .plt  exec

; PLT[0]: resolver stub
plt0:
    push    qword [rel got_plt+8]    ; push link_map
    jmp     qword [rel got_plt+16]   ; jmp _dl_runtime_resolve

; PLT[1]: printf stub (16 bytes)
plt_printf:
    jmp     qword [rel printf_got]   ; first call: jmps back to printf_push
plt_printf_push:
    push    0                        ; relocation index for printf
    jmp     plt0                     ; call resolver

; PLT[2]: puts stub (16 bytes)
plt_puts:
    jmp     qword [rel puts_got]
plt_puts_push:
    push    1                        ; relocation index for puts
    jmp     plt0

required_components.name: .rela.plt Section
required_components.description: Relocation table for PLT GOT slots. One R_X86_64_JUMP_SLOT entry per imported PLT function. The dynamic linker processes these entries to fill in the .got.plt slots.

code_example.source:
; === .rela.plt Section (Elf64_Rela, 24 bytes each) ===
section .rela.plt
rela_plt:
    ; Relocation for printf GOT slot
    dq printf_got_vaddr         ; r_offset: VA of printf's .got.plt slot
    dq (1 << 32) | 7            ; r_info:   (dynsym index 1) | R_X86_64_JUMP_SLOT
    dq 0                        ; r_addend

    ; Relocation for puts GOT slot
    dq puts_got_vaddr
    dq (2 << 32) | 7
    dq 0

required_components.name: Entry Point Code (_start with libc)
required_components.description: Entry point for a dynamically linked binary that calls libc functions through the PLT. Can call printf, puts, etc. via PLT stubs. Must call exit() or SYS_exit_group to terminate.

code_example.source:
; === 64-bit Dynamic Entry Point calling libc via PLT ===
; Link: ld program.o -o program -lc --dynamic-linker /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2
section .data
    fmt     db 'Hello, World!', 10, 0

section .text
global  _start
extern  printf
extern  exit

_start:
    lea     rdi, [rel fmt]      ; first arg: format string
    xor     eax, eax            ; no floating-point args (x86-64 ABI)
    call    printf wrt ..plt    ; call via PLT stub

    xor     edi, edi            ; exit code = 0
    call    exit wrt ..plt

variants.name: ELF64 64-bit Position-Independent Executable (ET_DYN / PIE)
variants.description: Position-Independent Executable built as e_type = ET_DYN (shared object). Enables full ASLR: the kernel loads the binary at a randomized base address. Required by modern Linux distributions. All symbol references use RIP-relative addressing or GOT-relative accesses.

required_components.name: ELF Header with e_type = ET_DYN
required_components.description: Identical to elf64_dynamic except e_type = ET_DYN (3) instead of ET_EXEC (2). The DT_FLAGS_1 entry in .dynamic should include DF_1_PIE to signal that this is an executable, not a library.

code_example.source:
; ELF Header change for PIE:
    dw 3    ; e_type = ET_DYN  (was 2 = ET_EXEC)

required_components.name: DT_FLAGS_1 = DF_1_PIE in .dynamic
required_components.description: Adding DT_FLAGS_1 with value DF_1_PIE (0x08000000) informs the dynamic linker and tools (e.g. file, execstack) that this ET_DYN file is an executable, not a shared library.

code_example.source:
; Add to .dynamic section (before DT_NULL):
    dq 0x6FFFFFFB, 0x08000000   ; DT_FLAGS_1 = DF_1_PIE

required_components.name: RIP-relative addressing for all data references
required_components.description: Because the load address is randomised, all global variable and string accesses must use RIP-relative (PC-relative) addressing. Absolute 64-bit addresses are not valid in PIE code without a relocation entry.

code_example.source:
; === RIP-relative data access in PIE code ===
section .data
    msg     db 'Hello PIE!', 10, 0

section .text
global  _start
extern  puts

_start:
    ; CORRECT: RIP-relative (works regardless of load address)
    lea     rdi, [rel msg]      ; load address of msg relative to RIP
    call    puts wrt ..plt

    ; WRONG for PIE: absolute address (assembler would emit a relocation
    ; that many kernels reject for ET_DYN executable code):
    ; mov rdi, msg              ; do NOT use this in PIE

    mov     rax, 231            ; SYS_exit_group
    xor     rdi, rdi
    syscall

variants.name: ELF32 32-bit Static Executable (ET_EXEC, no shared libraries)
variants.description: 32-bit Linux static executable using the INT 0x80 system call interface. ELF header is 52 bytes, Program Header entries are 32 bytes. System call arguments go in EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI, EBP.

required_components.name: ELF Header (ELF32)
required_components.description: 52-byte ELF32 header. Addresses and offsets are 4 bytes (Elf32_Addr / Elf32_Off). EI_CLASS = 1 (ELFCLASS32). e_machine = 3 (EM_386).

fields.name: e_phoff
fields.note: Program Header Table at offset 52 (immediately after header)

code_example.source:
; === ELF32 Header (52 bytes at file offset 0x0000) ===
elf_header:
    db 0x7F, 'E', 'L', 'F'     ; magic
    db 1                        ; EI_CLASS  = ELFCLASS32
    db 1                        ; EI_DATA   = little-endian
    db 1                        ; EI_VERSION
    db 0                        ; EI_OSABI
    times 8 db 0                ; padding
    dw 2                        ; e_type    = ET_EXEC
    dw 3                        ; e_machine = EM_386
    dd 1                        ; e_version
    dd _start                   ; e_entry   (32-bit VA)
    dd phdr_table               ; e_phoff
    dd shdr_table               ; e_shoff
    dd 0                        ; e_flags
    dw 52                       ; e_ehsize
    dw 32                       ; e_phentsize
    dw PHDR_COUNT               ; e_phnum
    dw 40                       ; e_shentsize
    dw SHDR_COUNT               ; e_shnum
    dw SHSTRNDX                 ; e_shstrndx

required_components.name: PT_LOAD Segment (ELF32, 32 bytes)
required_components.description: ELF32 Program Header entry is 32 bytes. p_flags position differs from ELF64: flags are at offset 0x18 (after p_paddr) instead of offset 0x04.

fields.name: p_flags
fields.note: PF_R | PF_X — NOTE: p_flags is at offset 0x18 in ELF32 (vs 0x04 in ELF64)

code_example.source:
; === ELF32 PT_LOAD (rx) Program Header Entry (32 bytes) ===
phdr_rx:
    dd 1                        ; p_type   = PT_LOAD
    dd 0                        ; p_offset (include ELF header in segment)
    dd 0x08048000               ; p_vaddr  (conventional 32-bit base)
    dd 0x08048000               ; p_paddr
    dd RX_FILE_SIZE             ; p_filesz
    dd RX_FILE_SIZE             ; p_memsz
    dd 5                        ; p_flags  = PF_R | PF_X (NOTE: position differs from ELF64!)
    dd 0x1000                   ; p_align  (4 KB)

required_components.name: Entry Point Code (_start, 32-bit INT 0x80 ABI)
required_components.description: 32-bit Linux system calls use INT 0x80. System call number in EAX; arguments in EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI, EBP. Return value in EAX.

code_example.source:
; === Minimal 32-bit Static Entry Point (INT 0x80) ===
; Assemble: nasm -f elf32 program.asm -o program.o
; Link:     ld -m elf_i386 program.o -o program
section .data
    msg     db 'Hello, World!', 10
    msglen  equ $ - msg

section .text
global _start

_start:
    ; write(1, msg, msglen)  -- syscall 4
    mov     eax, 4          ; SYS_write
    mov     ebx, 1          ; fd = stdout
    mov     ecx, msg        ; buf
    mov     edx, msglen     ; count
    int     0x80

    ; exit(0) -- syscall 1
    mov     eax, 1          ; SYS_exit
    xor     ebx, ebx        ; status = 0
    int     0x80

variants.name: ELF32 32-bit Dynamically Linked Executable (ET_EXEC, shared libraries)
variants.description: 32-bit Linux dynamically linked executable. Uses the same PT_INTERP, .dynamic, PLT/GOT mechanism as elf64_dynamic, but with 32-bit addresses and the x86 32-bit calling convention (stack-based arguments).

required_components.name: ELF32 Header + PT_INTERP + PT_DYNAMIC + PT_LOAD segments
required_components.description: Same logical structure as elf64_dynamic but all addresses and offsets are 4 bytes. PT_INTERP path is /lib/ld-linux.so.2.

required_components.name: PT_INTERP path (32-bit)
required_components.description: Interpreter path for 32-bit Linux dynamic linker.

code_example.source:
; .interp section for 32-bit dynamic linker
interp_str:
    db '/lib/ld-linux.so.2', 0

required_components.name: .rela.plt Entry (ELF32 uses Elf32_Rel not Elf32_Rela)
required_components.description: ELF32 typically uses REL (not RELA) relocations: 4-byte r_offset + 4-byte r_info, no addend. Relocation type for PLT slots is R_386_JMP_SLOT (7).

code_example.source:
; === ELF32 .rel.plt Entry (8 bytes, no addend) ===
rel_plt:
    ; Relocation for printf
    dd printf_got_vaddr         ; r_offset: VA of printf's GOT slot
    dd (1 << 8) | 7             ; r_info:   sym_index=1 | R_386_JMP_SLOT

required_components.name: Entry Point Code (_start, 32-bit dynamic)
required_components.description: 32-bit entry point calling libc functions. x86 calling convention: arguments pushed right-to-left on the stack.

code_example.source:
; === 32-bit Dynamic Entry Point calling libc ===
section .data
    msg     db 'Hello, World!', 10, 0

section .text
global  _start
extern  puts
extern  exit

_start:
    push    msg             ; argument: pointer to string (pushed on stack)
    call    puts wrt ..plt  ; call puts via PLT stub
    add     esp, 4          ; caller cleans the stack (cdecl convention)

    push    0               ; exit code = 0
    call    exit wrt ..plt
    add     esp, 4          ; (never reached)
